description: The lecture notes include an example (Example 6) about sampling 6 friends from 20 without replacement to count left-handed friends. This is technically a hypergeometric setting, not truly binomial, because the population (20 friends) is small relative to the sample (6), so trials are not independent. The notes appear to use it as a teaching example about the independence/population-size caveat, but the excerpt is cut off before resolution. Students may be confused about when an SRS from a small population violates the binomial setting.
suggested_resolution: Confirm with current-professor materials how Example 6 is resolved. If the course treats it as approximately binomial or uses it to illustrate a violation, add explicit guidance in the 'when to use' section. Flag for instructor review before publishing.

description: The formula sheet includes the Normal approximation to the binomial (X ~approx N(np, sqrt(np(1-p))) when np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10) as well as a z-score formula for binomial counts. This content is covered in M5D3 Notes and is on both exam formula sheets, so it is in scope. However, it bridges binomial and normal distribution topics. Ensure the study app treats the Normal approximation as part of the binomial topic or clearly links it to the Normal distributions topic to avoid student confusion.
suggested_resolution: Include Normal approximation as a sub-section of the binomial topic (as done here) and cross-link to the Normal distribution topic page.

description: The Exam 2 formula sheet does not include TI calculator functions for the binomial, while the Final Exam formula sheet does include binompdf and binomcdf. Students preparing for Exam 2 should be aware that TI functions may not be listed on that formula sheet, even if they can still use them.
suggested_resolution: Note in the app that TI functions appear on the Final Exam formula sheet. Verify with current-professor materials whether TI calculators are permitted on Exam 2 and whether students are expected to know the TI function names for that exam.
